sections.title: Fats
sections.narration: fat is 33.1g. omega 3 is doing serious work here. for dairy, the fat side can either add richness or drag the whole profile down.

sections.narration: carbs is 1.3g. sugar, glycemic index, and starch are doing most of the work. for dairy, the best versions keep the convenience without wrecking the fat profile.

sections.title: Proteins
sections.narration: protein is 24.9g. protein quality is clearly one of the selling points. for dairy, useful protein can rescue a lot of weaker traits.

sections.title: Vitamins
sections.narration: vitamin b12 at 30% DV, vitamin a at 30% DV stand out most. that helps, but vitamins still are not the main selling point.

sections.title: Minerals
sections.narration: calcium at 72% DV, zinc at 21% DV, magnesium at 7% DV stand out most. that is useful, but still not a huge mineral edge.

sections.title: Pros
sections.narration: the upsides first. very strong calcium identity because this is one of the cleanest reasons cheese still performs well inside dairy despite its drawbacks. protein density is strong because cheddar clearly still delivers on the protein side of dairy. very practical and versatile because it is genuinely easy to use in lots of foods.

sections.title: Cons
sections.narration: on the weaker side. saturated fat is a major drawback because this is the biggest reason cheddar is not an easy elite dairy food. energy density is harsh because calories add up quickly compared with cleaner dairy anchors. easy to overeat because highly palatable and dense enough to add up fast.

closing.summary: Cheddar Cheese stands out for very strong calcium identity and protein density is strong. but it also gets held back by saturated fat is a major drawback and energy density is harsh. Best when the protein payoff or fermentation angle outweighs the fat and sodium tradeoffs.
